turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This is an employment discrimination case. It was filed in the Federal District Court in Delaware by Mr. Ricks alleging unlawful discrimination based on his national origin. There really is a narrow question before the Court, when does the statute of limitations period run on the two statutes in issue? And those two statutes are Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and 42 United States Code 1981.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Rodriguez, perhaps at the outset you agency, the state Department of Labor. And that triggered the 300-day extension for EEOC filing. Now, there are myriad decisions of the lower courts concerning this of which I am sure you are aware. They go from one extreme of saying that if you file anywhere you're entitled 300 days; that's one part of it. The other end is that you must have a state filing which is timely, which this was not. Delaware was 120 days.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So it wasn't a timely state filing?

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: It was not a timely state filing.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: That could be very true and that's the reason I'm glad it was brought up here, Mr. Justice White, because if it was plain error, then, very obviously, you must rule on it. But as I said, there seems to be no decision in the lower courts concerning this, and in our particular case I really don't think it makes that much of a difference. And I'd be glad to cover that later but --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could it be plain error?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The Government amicus does think it makes a difference. So that injects another issue.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Mr. Justice Stewart, the District Court decided that the date that triggered the statute of

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In the District Court. And that's a three- year statute of limitations.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Yes. It's three years under Delaware, Delaware laws.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: The District Court chose the June 26, 1974 date, and that was the date that Mr. Ricks was notified that he would receive a terminal contract, and dismissed both actions under Title VII and also 1981 as being time-barred. The 3rd Circuit took the date of the end of the terminal con­

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is Mr. Ricks a United States citizen or not 7 employment acts and it sets those out as being compensation, terms of employment, conditions and privileges, and it places a time limitation and it says you must file within 180 days fron the alleged unlawful act. What the 3rd Circuit is doing is substituting for that and it's saying, you can wait until you're terminated or until you have a final decision to file. And it doesn't --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I was -- both the briefs of the parties and the amicus briefs kind of talk about this as an all-or-nothing situation, but it really depends, doesn't it, upon what the complaint is, what the complaint is? And if the complaint is for wrongful discharge, based upon a person's national origin, tion is, when was he terminated, isn't it?

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Yes, that's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if he alleges that he was denied tenure on account of his national origin, then the question is, when was he denied tenure?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That was the end of the line.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: — he was out of work, and -- but he did not have to accept that, that was up to him.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: But what my argument is, that the termination of his services is not the alleged unlawful act, the denial of tenure is.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But isn't that the question? Isn't that the dispositive question? What is the alleged discriminatory act ? violation. Now, our argument here is exactly the same, that Dr. Ricks was denied tenure March 13, 1974. That is barred by all statutes of limitations, 1981, Title VII. He —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Doesn't he also allege that it was con­ tinuing up till the time he was let go?

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Yes, he does, Mr. Justice Marshall, but --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It was continuing. Isn't that different from the Evans case?

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Well, Evans -- Evans, she left -- Very true --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: She couldn't have been persecuted when she wasn't there. Title VII. basic claim is that he was fired by reason of his national origin. Then the question is, was he fired and when? That's the first question.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The first two questions.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Under the faculty policy which he was subject to, when he was denied tenure on March 13, 1974, by the Board of Trustees, that was a final act.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if his claim of alleged discrimina­ tion is that he was denied tenure, then the question becomes, when was he denied tenure? number one, that he knows his employer's going to finally terminate him. That's the first test. And, number two -- and one could come before two or two could come after one -- he has to leave his employment. And I think that's the rule that is laid down in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, there can be violations of Title VII by somebody who never leaves the employment. I mean, the violation might be a failure to promote.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: That's our very point.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And there could be violations of Title VII by somebody who never becomes an employee. The violation might be a failure to hire. So, you can't have a general rule, can Stewart's suggestion, it depends a little bit on the way the an injunction requiring tenure, say, and just look at the damage action, I suppose normally a cause of action doesn't accrue until all the elements of the damage claimed are present. And he didn't suffer any monetary damage until he stopped working for the college, did he?

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: No, that's very true, Mr. Justice Stevens, but he took --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So wouldn't his damage claim have been premature if he'd brought suit before, while they still had him on the payroll?

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: I don't think so because his rights were terminated once the Board of Trustees denied tenure to him. ployed as of June 30, 1974, had he not decided to take the terminal contract. See, that had to be offered to him under the AAUP regulations. It was his decision to take that terminal contract or not, and —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, yes. And so, if he brought suit on January 1, 1975, say, he was still on the payroll.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But he knew that that terminal contract was only for one year.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Yes, he knew that that was -- June 30, 1975, he was done work. very explicitly, and it seems to me --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If the alleged discriminatory act is a failure to promote, based upon somebody's race, sex, or national origin, then that's the act, and it becomes important to deter­ mine, first, did that act occur? Secondly, when did it occur? And then the statute of limitations runs and then if you find that it is within the statute, then you consider the merits of the claim. But it has -- that alleged act has nothing to do with termination. the plaintiff —

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Yes, there can be no doubt about it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- of a certain sex or a certain race or a certain ethnic origin. But if that's not the gravamen of the complaint, then that that's not where the statute begins to run.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Another example, Mr. Justice, I'd like to bring out is the denial of seniority. That is a discrimina­ tory denial of seniority while one is employed is also a dis­ criminatory act and it could be sued upon when that occurs. Now, if that employee does not sue under the 3rd Circuit rules, does not bring an EEOC action and then, say, three or four years later they have a RIFing policy because of decrease in work force and because he was denied that promotion he doesn't

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think that the complaint stated a claim both for denial of a contract to teach and tenure?

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: The complaint originally listed six counts. And all counts were adjudged to be time-barred. Some -- I think two by agreement of counsel, and the rest by the court. And it covered the entire spectrum from denial of First Amendment rights to state rights under pendent jurisdiction, violation of terms of contract, things of that nature. Every­ thing was dismissed except these two actions. Now,in oral argument before Judge Stapleton, the district judge, Miss Harris indicated, I believe -- and I think she may contest that -- but Judge Stapleton assumed that there were two violations, denial of tenure and termination. And that is what he based think that's the reason that he picked the June 26, 1974, date, because as of that time Dr. Ricks had been denied tenure, which occurred by the Board of Trustees on March 13, 1974, and the significance of the June 26, 1974, date is a letter from the Board President indicating that he would be offered a terminal contract.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But when were his appeal rights exhausted on the tenure decision?

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: On the tenure decision? Would have --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: September?

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: He filed suit September 9, 1977, so it would have been September 9, 1974. The June date would have been prior to that. If you take 180 days, it would have been October 7, 1974. That's taking 180 days under the Title VII Grievance Committee reached a final decision on September 12, 1974. And our argument there is that this Court has held by your opinion in Electrical Workers that a grievance procedure does not toll the statute under Title VII. And the reasoning there was that the Congress did not provide for such a tolling and there are really two separate actions, the grievance pro­ cedure is contractual, Title VII is an Act of Congress. And that's our argument there for saying that the September 12, 1974, date is merely a decision on the grievance procedure. We feel that the decision is directly contrary to the purpose and intent of the statute of limitations, that if you permit someone to sue when they know they're going to be termi- the case with all due speed. siderations. It leaves the employer in a state of not knowing what's going to happen. He can have a termination four or five years from the date that it occurred, which could be promotion, denial of promotion, and then under the 3rd Circuit ruling suit could be brought at that time. That also leaves the employee in doubt. Does he file a Title VII as written, which says, 180 days from the date of the discriminatory act, or does he file under the 3rd Circuit ruling, that he can bring suit after he leaves the services of his employer?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't it possible that even though the 3rd Circuit was wrong in laying down a general rule, the plain­ tiff might prevail on this particular case? reason of his national origin, then he might prevail, because he was terminated from his employment, and if he can show that the reason he was terminated was because of his national origin, then he might prevail under Title VII, quite apart from 1981.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Well, Mr. Justice Stewart, our argu­ ment there is that he was terminated once tenure was denied because he --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, I thought your argument was that the only discriminatory act fairly alleged is a failure to give him tenure ?

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Yes, that's very true, but under the policy, once he was denied tenure for the second time, he was

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you disagree with that characteriza­ acterized, then he, as my brother Rehnquist suggested in his question, he might prevail for a reason other than the broad reasoning of Judge Higginbotham for the 3rd Circuit.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Yes, sir. But under the faculty regulations, Mr. Justice, he knew that the second time he was denied tenure, that he would no longer be employed by the college, and our argument is that that, if there was an alleged discriminatory act, it occurred then, because that sealed --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that would be the merits of the case.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: Yes. That sealed his fate, at that tion, when you phrase it that way.

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: I think it does, Mr. Justice Stevens.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If a tenure decision doesn't automatically result in termination, didn't the one year contract even more clearly indicate that he was terminated at the end of that year?

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: The denial of tenure, Mr. Justice White, does result in termination. There's no question about it. That's clearly in the policy that was just as much a part of Dr. Ricks' contract as if it had been written in there.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But what about the one year contract that was then given him?

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: That was entirely discretionary. The AAUP —

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: They had to offer that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But what if that were determined to be the discriminatory act? Would that have been timely or not?

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: No. That was a voluntary act by Dr. Ricks. He could accept the terminal contract for one year or he could reject it. He chose to accept it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He chose to accept it but it was also, it certainly was unequivocally indicated then that he was termi­ nated at the end of that year?

turns.name: NICHOLAS H. RODRIGUEZ
turns.text: No question about it. He knew as of March 13, 1974, when the Board of Trustees made the decision to deny tenure. I'd like to begin by answering a question posed by Justice Stewart concerning what it is that Dr. Ricks is claiming in his complaint. He is claiming that, and I think a fair read­ ing of his complaint indicates that he is claiming that his discharge is discriminatory for reasons of national origin.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's a little hard to -- maybe it is a fair reading, is it? I find the complaint a little hard to read.

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: I'm sorry, I didn't hear you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I find the complaint a little hard to read

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That remains to be proved. I would say a fair reading of the complaint says, yes; that he is alleging the discharge as a separate violation, separate discriminatory act. One of the problems obviously with this case is it's been ruled on on the pleadings alone. I think he is entitled to put forth his proof as to whether the discharge itself was discrim­ inatory, whether he was discharged in a manner in which those who were not of his national origin were not discharged.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even though they too had been denied tenure?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: Even though they too had been denied tenure. That's correct; yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, a lot of this is, or most of it is irrelevant, if you are correct on this 300-day submission.

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you as well as an amicus support that and so as a respondent you are urging this in support of the decision beloW?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That's correct. I believe that he filec his charge within 180 days. I believe that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: All right. But I believe that if this 180 days. It makes specific reference to Olson v. Rembrandt Publishing Company, I believe. But it doesn't say -- it says clearly that it doesn't have to be filed within 180 days, and it suggests in that footnote that the charge doesn't have to be filed timely with the state. I would also like to point out, Justice Rehnquist, that there's some question as to whether Dr. Ricks's charge was untimely with the state. What the state did with that charge was to waive jurisdiction over to the EEOC, and I'd submit that the waiver of jurisdiction, inherent in that waiver is the power September 3, 1974. Those two dates are within the 300-day period, if you assume the Mohasco reading. In addition, the College issued to Dr. Ricks a notice that tenure had in fact been denied. After the review of the educational policy decision, the Board had decided to deny him tenure and that notice was issued on September 12, 1974. Again, for purposes of Title VII, if you assume the Mohasco treatment of the case and the 300 days applies, that September 12, 1974, date is within the 300-day period. I might also add that it's within the three-year period for purposes of Section 1981. We both agree, and both the District Court and the Court of Appeals held that it was a three-year Delaware statute of liir l- tations that applied to the 19 81 claim, that the Court complaint insofar as the person who allegedly engaged in that conduct subsequently evaluated him in that last year of the contract.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that might be evidentiary but it's not itself a substantive cause of action under Title VII.

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That's correct. No, no, it's not alleged as a cause of action under Title VII. It is an eviden­ tiary matter.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Miss Harris, before you go on, could we come back to the complaint for a minute or two? I had thought the charge was related solely to the denial of tenure. Then, if I understand that you are saying that actually the denial was based on an unlawful discharge. Is that correct? criminatory and in violation of Section 1981. And that would

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Apart from that, there’s no specific aver­ ment of discriminatory discharge?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That said that -- some allegations separate and apart from the tenure?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. There are 15 or 16 paragraphs that talk about tenure, so I was under the impression until this morning that your contention was based on the denial of tenure, but I’ll take another look at it.

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: I think a fair reading of the complaint is that most of the allegations do go to tenure; yes, that's correct. There are no allegations separate and apart from those previous denial of tenure required the issuance of a terminal contract and certain treatment prescribed in the College's regulations, that did not in fact occur. That being the prior case, I think it remains a question as to whether he would have automatically ceased to be employed with the College simply because he was awarded what's called the terminal contract. In addition a --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It was a one year contract?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: Yes, it was.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And there was no offer of a second one year tenure and a series of one year contracts, isn't it? I mean, teachers generally who do not have tenure are operating under a year-to-year contract and then when they get tenure they no longer have to depend on a one year contract.

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: Yes, Justice Rehnquist, that's my under­ standing of the practice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I suppose a nontenured teacher, though, could have a five-year contract?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: I suppose so. I don't know that that's been the experience at Delaware State College, but --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: As an instructor or an assistant professor, or whatever.

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That's probably true. tenure he was to be afforded a terminal contract, and that was deemed to be the terminal contract.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that would mean that he would not be working for the university after the end of that terminal contract ?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: Well, I think that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, otherwise it isn't terminal?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That's correct. I don't think, though, that the issuance of the contract necessarily meant that he would not be working there after June 30, 1975, and I say that -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What does terminal mean?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That's what it ordinarily means but I would say, Justice White, that the -- the letter was if the Board of Trustees granted him tenure.

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That's correct. And by September 12, '74, that had happened.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is this in your view just a traditional method of giving the faculty member a year's time to find new employment, and a year's notice, in effect, that at the end of the year there would be no more employment at this institution? Is that what it amounts to?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: The issuance of a terminal contract, Mr. Chief Justice? — Yes, it is. I think it's a very common practice in academic institutions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is it fundamentally any different from ordinary workers in industry that might get 15-day or 30-day than the beginning of the notice period. Isn't that the essence of it ?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: Yes. The end of -- the point at which he ceases to work. In this case it was the end of the notice period.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Not the point of termination of the con­ tractual relationship?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That's correct. That is my position.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Getting back to Justice Powell's question, it seems to be clear, at least in the first sentence of Judge Higginbotham's opinion for the Court of Appeals for the 3rd Circuit that that court considered your complaint to allege a discriminatory denial of tenure. And "denial of tenure," that

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That's right. there seems to be a possibility that it was a 300-day statute. Should we remand it to the Court of Appeals to decide that question? Because the answer to the question seems to depend upon whether or not there was a filing in the state, with the state commission.

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: Yes, I think you would be required to do that, and that this Court should not decide that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Only if we think your 180-day argument is -

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: — is incorrect. That's correct. Yes, Justice Brennan.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's right. If you win, even under the 18 0 days, a fortiori you'd win under the 3:00? I think that that's what happened in I.U.E. vs. Robbins S Myers, where the question was whether a grievance procedure tolled the Title VII time limitation. In that case this Court held that the time for filing the charge ran from the last day that the employee worked and not from the later point when the grievance was concluded, a point at which he was off the payroll

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think this situation is analogous to a grievance procedure?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: No, I'm simply saying that — no, I don't. I don't at all. I think that this situation is simply a question of focusing on when the discharge occurred, from the perspective of the respondent. And I believe that that discharge and what's commonly understood in Title VII law to be suffered by an employer in this situation because employers naturally maintain records of employees that they have on the rolls. They can defend. The claim really isn't stale. If you're talking about the termination of employment, they can defend, they have the records available, and they know that they only have to defend up to either 180 days or, if this Court so decides, 300 days of the date of discharge, so that the claim really isn't stale. Attention's been called to the fact that two of the witnesses are unavailable. One of them is deceased -- one died college archives or in the college records, and used in support of the college's position that denial of tenure was appropriate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Might there not be considerable evidence that wouldn't be a matter of record, something in people's memories ?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That could be. Yes, I would have to say that that's possible but it might be possible for colleges to simply hold their proceedings as they did here in the Educa­ tional Policy Committee. There were transcripts of a hearing made, and witnesses came in and they got to do it in the form of testimony, and to submit evidence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Your positions apparently equate the exis­ tence of a contract with the execution of it. That is, you're natory act and that notice was not given under similar circum­ origin. It may very well be that notice was given to everyone in those circumstances. On the other hand --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't that a little difficult to square with the general idea of staleness in the prosecution of claims? Take the five-year illustration that was suggested hypotheti­ cally by Justice Rehnquist. It's a pretty stale claim if the statute doesn't begin to run until the end of the five years of actual work, is it not?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: I don't believe so, if the allegation is that the discharge, the ultimate act of terminating the discriminatory. Would you still say that the statute ran from the time he last occupied a desk in the university?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: Oh, that's a harder problem. That looks more like the effects of discrimination than it does an active discriminatory act.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Mr. Rodriguez says that's precisely this case.

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: I don't agree that that is this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Miss Harris, I want to be sure about one thing. The charge was filed after 180 days but still while he was in the employment of the college.

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Blackmun.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So under your theory, then, the charge a position and asked to train someone else, and the court found in that case that the time really started to run from the point at which that person assumed the job.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, are you defending the rationale of the Court of Appeals or not?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: The rationale of the 3rd Circuit? Yes, I am.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, this Court of Appeals said the dis­ criminatory act was a continuing one.

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: No, it said the process of termination was a continuing process.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right, but it was a process. And when in 1974, resulting in an early discharge in 1990, with nothing happening in between. And under that rationale, it seems to me that the 1990 claim would be timely. Do I misread the opinion?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: No, I don't believe a 1990 claim would be timely if there's no difference between the discriminatory denial of seniority and tenure. I think they would be treated the same but I don't think the 1990 claim would be timely.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, under the logic of the Court of Appeals' opinion, my brother Stevens suggests that it would be.

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: Yes, I understand that, but I believe that the 1990 claim, to the extent that it alleged a discrimi­ natory denial of seniority resulting in a layoff or an earlier

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That’s correct; they did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And I’m assuming, in my seniority case, continuous employment. And so I don't think you can answer it by saying, well, Evans would apply there. I don't -- if Evans applies there, in other words, I think it may well apply here. That's what I --

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That's correct. I was assuming -- I misunderstood your hypothetical. I was assuming that the employment had terminated. I jumped the gun; I'm sorry. I think that the Court of Appeals opinion, in thinking about it, in both instances would say that the 1990 charge was a timely charge.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, I think they would. because someone else was put into the position, or precluded from getting a higher salary because that job grade, for example, might have been upgraded and people who were higher level than he or she would get a raise that that person would not get. I think it's the point at which the adverse --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You really must take that position to sup­ port your other positions, must you not?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: Yes, I believe I must, and I do.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But in a small plant, a small industrial operation, that might be a long, long time before there was any impact, would it not, could be?

turns.name: HARRIS
turns.text: That's right. It could be. Could very well be, in a very static employment situation. 46 CERTIFICATE North American Reporting hereby certifies that the attached pages represent an accurate transcript of electronic sound recording in the oral argument before the Supreme Court of the
